返回本批次

您可以从表格中获得多少精度？

How much precision can you squeeze out of a table?

www.johndcook.com 作者 nomemory 分数 48 评论 6 2026/03/27 03:47
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 理查德·费曼说，如果你研究得足够深入，几乎一切都会变得有趣。在表格中查找数字当然并不有趣，但是当您深入了解如何填补空白时，它会变得更有趣。如果您想知道表中给出的 x 值之间的列表函数值，则必须使用插值。线性插值通常就足够了，但使用高阶插值可以获得更准确的结果。假设您有一个在 x = 3.00, 3.01, 3.02, …, 3.99, 4.00 处列出的函数 f(x)，并且您想要近似该函数在 π 处的值。您可以使用 f(3.14) 和 f(3.15) 的值通过线性插值来近似 f(π)，但您也可以利用表中的更多点。例如，您可以使用三次插值法使用 f(3.13)、f(3.14)、f(3.15) 和 f(3.16) 计算 f(π)。或者您可以使用 29 度插值，其中 f 的值为 3.00、3.01、3.02、…、3.29。拉格朗日插值定理可让您计算插值误差的上限。然而，该定理假设每个列表点的值都是准确的。对于普通使用，您可以假设表格中的值是准确的。最大的误差源通常是列表 x 值之间的差距大小，而不是列表值的精度。表格的设计就是为了证明这一点是正确的 [1]。 n 阶插值误差的界限具有 c hn + 1 + λ δ 的形式，其中 h 是插值点之间的间距，δ 是表格值中的误差。 c 的值取决于您要插值的函数的导数 [2]。 λ 的值至少为 1，因为 λδ 是表格点处的“插值”误差。一般来说，插值的精度不可能优于 δ，因此您选择的 n 值应使 c hn + 1 小于 δ。任何更高的 n 值都没有帮助。事实上，较高的 n 值是有害的，因为 λ 作为 n 的函数呈指数增长[3]。有关 λ 的数学详细信息，请参阅下一篇文章。例子让我们看一个具体的例子。这是 A&S 的自然对数表的一部分。
  2. 这里 h = 10−3，因此线性插值会给您带来 h² = 10−6 数量级的误差。您永远不会得到小于 10−15 的误差，因为这是表格值中的误差，因此四阶插值可为您提供与您将获得的精度一样多的精度。仔细限制误差需要使用上面特定于此上下文的 c 和 λ 值。事实上，使用 5 阶插值时，插值误差约为 10−8，这是您能做到的最好结果。我将简要提及 A&S 的几个例子。本书包括一个正弦值表，制表至小数点后 23 位，增量为 h = 0.001 弧度。粗略估计表明 7 阶插值与您应该达到的一样高，事实上，书上指出 7 阶插值将为您提供 9 位数字的精度，A&S 的另一个表格给出了贝塞尔函数 J0 的值，其中 15 位数字的值，增量为 h = 0.1。它表示 11 阶插值将为您提供四位小数的精度。在这种情况下，相当高阶的插值是有用的，甚至是必要的。相对于输出精度，表格值中需要大量小数位，因为点之间的间距太宽。相关帖子
  3. 使用对数 Bessel、Everett 和 Lagrange 插值表
  4. [1] 我说是因为人们当然很少再在表中查找函数值了。表格和插值仍然被广泛使用，只是人们不直接使用；计算机代表它们进行查找和插值。 [2] 对于像正弦这样的函数，c 的值不会随着 n 的增加而增加，事实上，c 的值会随着 n 的增加而缓慢减小。但对于其他函数，c 可以随 n 增长，这可能会导致诸如龙格现象之类的问题。 [2] 对于均匀分布的插值点，常数 λ 随 n 呈指数增长，并且表中的值是均匀分布的。对于切比雪夫间距，常数 λ 仅以对数方式增长，但这对于通用表来说并不实用。

Original Summary

  1. Richard Feynman said that almost everything becomes interesting if you look into it deeply enough. Looking up numbers in a table is certainly not interesting, but it becomes more interesting when you dig into how well you can fill in the gaps. If you want to know the value of a tabulated function between values of x given in the table, you have to use interpolation. Linear interpolation is often adequate, but you could get more accurate results using higher-order interpolation. Suppose you have a function f(x) tabulated at x = 3.00, 3.01, 3.02, …, 3.99, 4.00 and you want to approximate the value of the function at π. You could approximate f(π) using the values of f(3.14) and f(3.15) with linear interpolation, but you could also take advantage of more points in the table. For example, you could use cubic interpolation to calculate f(π) using f(3.13), f(3.14), f(3.15), and f(3.16). Or you could use 29th degree interpolation with the values of f at 3.00, 3.01, 3.02, …, 3.29. The Lagrange interpolation theorem lets you compute an upper bound on your interpolation error. However, the theorem assumes the values at each of the tabulated points are exact. And for ordinary use, you can assume the tabulated values are exact. The biggest source of error is typically the size of the gap between tabulated x values, not the precision of the tabulated values. Tables were designed so this is true [1]. The bound on order n interpolation error has the form c hn + 1 + λ δ where h is the spacing between interpolation points and δ is the error in the tabulated values. The value of c depends on the derivatives of the function you’re interpolating [2]. The value of λ is at least 1 since λδ is the “interpolation” error at the tabulated points. The accuracy of an interpolated value cannot be better than δ in general, and so you pick the value of n that makes c hn + 1 less than δ. Any higher value of n is not helpful. And in fact higher values of n are harmful since λ grows exponentially as a function of n [3]. See the next post for mathematical details regarding the λs. Examples Let’s look at a specific example. Here’s a piece of a table for natural logarithms from A&S.
  2. Here h = 10−3, and so linear interpolation would give you an error on the order of h² = 10−6. You’re never going to get error less than 10−15 since that’s the error in the tabulated values, so 4th order interpolation gives you about as much precision as you’re going to get. Carefully bounding the error would require using the values of c and λ above that are specific to this context. In fact, the interpolation error is on the order of 10−8 using 5th order interpolation, and that’s the best you can do. I’ll briefly mention a couple more examples from A&S. The book includes a table of sine values, tabulated to 23 decimal places, in increments of h = 0.001 radians. A rough estimate would suggest 7th order interpolation is as high as you should go, and in fact the book indicates that 7th order interpolation will give you 9 figures of accuracy, Another table from A&S gives values of the Bessel function J0 in with 15 digit values in increments of h = 0.1. It says that 11th order interpolation will give you four decimal places of precision. In this case, fairly high-order interpolation is useful and even necessary. A large number of decimal places are needed in the tabulated values relative to the output precision because the spacing between points is so wide. Related posts
  3. Using a table of logarithms Bessel, Everett, and Lagrange interpolation
  4. [1] I say were because of course people rarely look up function values in tables anymore. Tables and interpolation are still widely used, just not directly by people; computers do the lookup and interpolation on their behalf. [2] For functions like sine, the value of c doesn’t grow with n, and in fact decreases slowly as n increases. But for other functions, c can grow with n, which can cause problems like Runge phenomena. [2] The constant λ grows exponentially with n for evenly spaced interpolation points, and values in a table are evenly spaced. The constant λ grows only logarithmically for Chebyshev spacing, but this isn’t practical for a general purpose table.

评论（默认中文）

这都是关于您手动进行的数值分析，并介绍了许多非常酷的数学知识，例如微分法

When I was in high school I thought book this was so much fun

http://182.160.97.198:8080/xmlui/bitstream/handle/123456789/...

which is all about the kind of numerical analysis you would do by hand and introduces a lot of really cool math like the calculus of differences

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Finite_difference

Rendello 2026/03/27 11:00
书内的版式和设计很漂亮，但封面看起来像旧的廉价商店传单。有一种说法是这样的，即以貌取人？我记不太清了。
The typography and design inside the book is beautiful but the cover looks like an old bargain-store flyer. There's an expression about that, something about judging a book by its cover? I can't remember exactly.

yababa_y 2026/03/27 07:40
我一直缺少这些知识！谢谢你的推荐。
i've been missing this knowledge! thank you for the recommendation.
在 HN 中查看
gus_massa 2026/03/27 04:25
吹毛求疵：

> 你永远不会得到小于 10E−15 的误差，因为这是表格值中的误差，

如果表中有 100 个（或 400 个）值，您可以再压缩一位数字。

在简单的情况下，假设您有 15 位数字的常数 pi，重复 100 次。如果舍入是在随机方向上进行的，例如

  floor(pi * 1E15 + random() - 1/2 ) / 1E15
那么您可以使用统计量来获得平均值，其误差类似于 K/sqrt(N)，其中 K 是常数，N 是样本数。如果你在统计课上注意过，你可能知道 K 的值，但我的情况不是这样。不管怎样，只要选择 N=100 或 N=400 或类似的东西，你就会得到另一个“免费”的数字。

没有人为常量构建表格并使用统一的偏移量进行舍入。无论如何，对于一些在样本点没有特殊值的平滑函数，剪切小数的确切值是相当“随机”的，并且您会得到类似的随机化。

Nitpicking:

> You’re never going to get error less than 10E−15 since that’s the error in the tabulated values,

If you have like 100 (or 400) values in the table, you can squeeze one more digit.

In the simple case, imagine you have the constant pi, with 15 digits, repeated 100 times. If the rounding was done in a random direction like

  floor(pi * 1E15 + random() - 1/2 ) / 1E15
then you can use statistic to get an average with an error that is like K/sqrt(N) where K is a constant and N is the number of samples. If you were paying attention in you statistic clases, you probably know the value of K, but it's not my case. Anyway, just pick N=100 or N=400 or something like that and you get another digit for "free".

Nobody builds a table for a constant and uses a uniform offset for rounding. Anyway, with some smooth function that has no special values in the points where it's sample, the exact value of the cut decimal is quite "random" and you get a similar randomization.

在 HN 中查看
_0ffh 2026/03/27 07:53
此外，头脑正常的人也不会使用查找表，其中表值实际上是真实 f(x) 的浮点近似值 - 您选择支持值来最小化 x 上插值的密集采样的误差（例如 mse）（或者，在极限情况下，真实曲线与支持插值之间所选误差函数的积分）。如果你想例如使用线性插值近似凸函数，所有表格值 f'(x) 将 <= 真实的 f(x)。
Also nobody in his right mind uses lookup tables where the table value is actually the float approximation of the true f(x) - you choose the support values to minimize an error (e.g. mse) of a dense sampling of your interpolated value over x (or, in the limit, the integral of the chosen error function between the true curve and the interpolation of your supports). If you want to e.g. approximate a convex function using linear interpolation, all the tabulated values f'(x) would be <= the true f(x).
在 HN 中查看
AlotOfReading 2026/03/27 12:36
在很多情况下，真实值更有用。例如，如果您正在构建一个由浮点的高尾数位索引的表，则很难将误差正确地分布在所有间隔上。
The true value is far more useful in a lot of cases. If you're building a table indexed by the upper mantissa bits of the float, for example, it's difficult to distribute the error properly across all intervals.
在 HN 中查看
